title: Twenty years of UN resolutions – and the Rohingya still wait
gist: After 20 years and 40+ UN resolutions, the international community has grown more effective at pursuing justice for the Rohingya but worse at sustaining protection — the crisis's center of gravity has shifted from Myanmar's atrocities to the eroding politics of protection across Bangladesh and Southeast Asia.

insights.claim: Over 20 years the international community has become more effective at pursuing justice for the Rohingya but far less successful at sustaining their protection.
insights.speaker: Yuyun Wahyuningrum
insights.so_what: Legal accountability and on-the-ground protection are diverging — the binding gap is political, not legal.
insights.quote: The international community has become more effective at pursuing justice, but far less successful at sustaining protection.
insights.locator: Twenty years of Council engagement, therefore

insights.claim: The UN Human Rights Council has adopted more than 40 resolutions on Myanmar since 2006, about half focused on the Rohingya, and built institutions (2017 Fact-Finding Mission, 2018 Investigative Mechanism) making it one of the world's most documented atrocity situations.
insights.speaker: Yuyun Wahyuningrum
insights.so_what: Documentation is exhaustive; the constraint is enforcement and protection, not evidence.
insights.locator: The Council has now adopted more than 40 resolutions

insights.claim: Rohingya accountability now spans the ICJ (Gambia's 2019 genocide case), an ICC investigation with 2024 and 2025 arrest-warrant applications, and Argentina's 2025 universal-jurisdiction case against Min Aung Hlaing.
insights.speaker: Yuyun Wahyuningrum
insights.so_what: A multi-layered legal net is tightening around Myanmar's leadership — a slow-burn legitimacy constraint.
insights.locator: This is illustrated by the ICC's subsequent

themes.theme: Crisis center of gravity shifts regionally

insights.claim: The greatest obstacles to protecting the Rohingya now lie across the region rather than inside Myanmar, having become a 'politics of protection' problem.
insights.speaker: Yuyun Wahyuningrum
insights.so_what: Solutions must target host-state politics, not just Myanmar's junta — a reframing of the crisis.
insights.locator: But the centre of gravity of the crisis has transformed

insights.claim: Bangladesh hosts more than one million Rohingya amid shrinking aid, deteriorating camps and rising host-community tensions intensifying repatriation pressure, while continuing Rakhine violence including junta air strikes near the border means safe return conditions are absent.
insights.speaker: Yuyun Wahyuningrum
insights.so_what: Repatriation pressure with no safe-return conditions is a building humanitarian and stability risk on the Bangladesh border.
insights.locator: Bangladesh illustrates how the crisis has shifted

insights.claim: Support for the Rohingya is eroding across Malaysia, Indonesia (Aceh) and Thailand via economic pressure, disinformation, solidarity fatigue and border-security framing that favours detention and maritime pushbacks over protection.
insights.speaker: Yuyun Wahyuningrum
insights.so_what: Regional public-opinion hardening reduces the political space for refugee protection across Southeast Asia.
insights.locator: In Southeast Asia, support for the Rohingya

insights.claim: Rising xenophobia, donor fatigue, shrinking humanitarian budgets, misinformation and domestic political pressures now shape the Rohingya's future as much as events inside Myanmar.
insights.speaker: Yuyun Wahyuningrum
insights.so_what: Funding-fatigue and xenophobia are the leading indicators for the crisis trajectory, not battlefield events alone.
